elements.sent-2411-001: Spin polarization and spin transport are common phenomena in many quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Relativistic spin hydrodynamics provides an effective low-energy framework to describe these processes in quantum many-body systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: The fundamental symmetry underlying relativistic spin hydrodynamics is angular momentum conservation, which naturally leads to inter-conversion between spin and orbital angular momenta.
elements.sent-2411-004: This inter-conversion is a key feature of relativistic spin hydrodynamics, closely related to entropy production and introducing ambiguity in the construction of constitutive relations.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this article, we present a pedagogical introduction to relativistic spin hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate how to derive the constitutive relations by applying local thermodynamic laws and explore several distinctive aspects of spin hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-007: These include the pseudo-gauge ambiguity, the behavior of the system in the presence of strong vorticity, and the challenges of modeling the freeze-out of spin in heavy-ion collisions.
elements.sent-2411-008: We also outline some future prospects for spin hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-009: Spin is a fundamental property of particles arising from quantum mechanics, playing a central role in numerous phenomena within the quantum regime.
elements.sent-2411-010: As a form of angular momentum, spin naturally couples to rotation, allowing it to become polarized by rotational motion.
elements.sent-2411-011: Similarly, for a charged particle with nonzero spin, or a neutral particle with a non-trivial charge form factor, spin can couple to an external magnetic field as well.
elements.sent-2411-012: Additionally, for a particle in motion (i.e., with finite momentum), its spin may couple to acceleration, electric fields, or gradients of external potentials, such as a chemical potential and temperature.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the case of massless particles, the spin state is specified by its helicity state, meaning it is intrinsically slaved to the particle's motion.
elements.sent-2411-014: As a result, spin can be manipulated by rotating fields, magnetic fields, electric fields, and a number of other external influences.
elements.sent-2411-015: Conversely, detecting the spin of a particle provides invaluable insights into the environment or the underlying dynamics of the system.
elements.sent-2411-016: In heavy-ion collision physics, the primary interest lies in the creation of deconfined quark-gluon matter, commonly referred to as the quark-gluon plasma (QGP)~\cite{Wang:2016opj .}
elements.sent-2411-017: To uncover the properties of QGP in heavy-ion collision experiments, it is essential to design specific hadronic observables that are sensitive to particular features of the QGP.
elements.sent-2411-018: Since charged particles are typically the easiest to detect, many observables rely on the charge of the hadrons.
elements.sent-2411-019: For instance, the total multiplicity of detected charged hadrons reflects the initial energy of the QGP.
elements.sent-2411-020: Meanwhile, the anisotropy in the momentum-space distribution of charged hadrons corresponds to the initial anisotropy in the spatial distribution of partons, leading to the well-known harmonic flow parameters~\cite{Ollitrault:1992bk .}
elements.sent-2411-021: By measuring these hadronic observables, we have revealed several novel properties of the hot and dense matter created in heavy-ion collisions.
elements.sent-2411-022: One significant finding is that the QGP must be extremely hot, with a typical temperature reaching 300-500 MeV at RHIC and LHC, indicating an extremely large energy density.
elements.sent-2411-023: Additionally, the QGP medium is found to be strongly interacting, with a very small shear viscosity to entropy density ratio $\eta/s$.
elements.sent-2411-024: This low ratio is required to explain the observed harmonic flow parameters~\cite{Wang:2016opj .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In fact, the $\eta/s$ of QGP is the lowest among all known fluids.
elements.sent-2411-026: Since 2017, it has been established that the spin degree of freedom can also be used to probe the properties of the QGP~\cite{STAR:2017ckg .}
elements.sent-2411-027: This is achieved through measurements of the spin polarization of spinful hadrons, such as hyperons and vector mesons~\cite{Liang:2004ph,Voloshin:2004ha,Liang:2004xn .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Notably, it has been observed that the $\Lambda$ and $\bar{\Lambda}$ hyperons can exhibit significant spin polarization at collision energies of tens of GeV~\cite{STAR:2017ckg,STAR:2018gyt,STAR:2021beb,HADES:2022enx,STAR:2023nvo .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Similarly, the $\phi$ and $J/\psi$ mesons have been found to exhibit considerable spin alignment~\cite{STAR:2022fan,ALICE:2022dyy ~\footnote{The spin alignment of a vector meson is quantified by the deviation of $\rho_{00}$ from $1/3$, where $\rho_{00}$ is the $00$-component of the vector meson's spin density matrix.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: These discoveries open new avenues for studying QGP through the spin degree of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-031: For instance, we now understand that the so-called global spin polarization (i.e., the total amount of spin polarization with respect to the reaction plane) of hyperons arises from angular momentum conservation through the formation of fluid vortices within the QGP: In non-central heavy-ion collisions, the system possesses substantial orbital angular momentum, which subsequently induces strong fluid vorticity in the QGP~\cite{Deng:2016gyh,Jiang:2016woz,Deng:2020ygd , thereby polarizing the spins of quarks via spin-rotation coupling~\cite{Becattini:2013fla,Fang:2016vpj,Liu:2020flb,Karpenko:2016jyx,Xie:2017upb,Li:2017slc,Shi:2017wpk,Xia:2018tes,Wei:2018zfb,Vitiuk:2019rfv,Ivanov:2019ern,Fu:2020oxj,Guo:2021uqc,Guo:2021udq,Li:2021zwq,Deng:2021miw,Wu:2022mkr .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, to fully understand the spin polarization phenomena, a dynamical theory of spin polarization and spin transport in the hot medium is essential, analogous to the necessity of a dynamical theory of the bulk medium for understanding harmonic flows.
elements.sent-2411-033: Naturally, such a dynamical theory of spin transport could be derived from either kinetic theory or hydrodynamic theory.
elements.sent-2411-034: In recent years, both spin kinetic theory and spin hydrodynamics have made significant advancements.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this article, we will focus on spin hydrodynamics, while we refer readers to Refs.~\cite{Hidaka:2022dmn for a review of spin kinetic theory and to Refs.~\cite{Gao:2020vbh,Becattini:2020ngo,Huang:2020dtn,Liu:2020ymh,Becattini:2022zvf,Jian-Hua:2023cna,Becattini:2024uha,Chen:2024afy for a review of spin polarization phenomena in heavy-ion collisions.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Throughout this article, we use the natural units $c=\hbar=k_B=0$ and the metric convention $\eta_{\m\n =\eta^{\m\n ={\rm diag (1,-1,-1,-1)$.}}}
elements.section-2411-002: Relativistic hydrodynamics as an effective theory
elements.sent-2411-037: Before we go into the discussion of spin hydrodynamics, let us first briefly review the general structure of relativistic hydrodynamics from the perspective of effective field theory.
elements.sent-2411-038: Hydrodynamic theory describes the low-energy behavior of interacting many-body systems, where only conserved charge densities exhibit their dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-039: Since conserved charge densities do not vanish, they simply redistribute themselves in space according to their equations of motion (EOMs).
elements.sent-2411-040: When expressed in a manner of spatial gradient expansion, these EOMs constitute the hydrodynamic equations.
elements.sent-2411-041: Let us consider the hydrodynamic theory of a system with spacetime translation symmetry and a global $U(1)$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-042: The corresponding conserved charge densities are the energy density $\ve(x)$, momentum density $\p^i(x), i=1-3$, and the $U(1)$ charge density $n(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-043: We want to derive the dynamical equations for these conserved charge densities.
elements.sent-2411-044: Sometimes it is more convenient to work with the potential variables conjugate to the charge densities.
elements.sent-2411-045: They are the temperature $T(x)$ (or its inverse $\b(x)=1/T(x)$), the fluid velocity $u^\m(x)$ normalized as $u^\m u^\n \eta_{\m\n =1$, and the chemical potential of $n(x)$, $\m(x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: These conserved charge densities (and equivalently their conjugates) are hydrodynamic variables in hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our starting point is the conservation laws: \begin{eqnarray}\label{con:tmn} \pt_\m \H^{\m\n}=0, \label{con:j} \pt_\m J^\m=0,\end{eqnarray} where $\H^{\m\n}$ is the energy-momentum tensor and $J^\m$ is the $U(1)$ current.
elements.sent-2411-048: As an effective field theory, we want to express $\H^{\m\n}$ and $J^\m$ in terms of the conserved charged density (or equivalently, their conjugates) and their various gradient orders.
elements.sent-2411-049: We assume spatial isotropy of the system, i.e., there is no external forces breaking the $SO(3)$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-050: The building blocks are the fluid velocity $u^\m$ and various quantities that can be classified into different representations of $SO(3)$ in the rest frame of the fluid.
elements.sent-2411-052: Note that the co-moving time derivatives will be eventually replaced by spatial gradients up on using the EOMs at leading order.
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that the vorticity tensor transform the same way as a three-vector under proper three-rotations (i.e., a three-rotation $R$ with $\det R=1$) as it can be substituted by a three pseudo-vector $\o^\m\equiv -(1/2)\e^{\m\n\r\s u_\n\o_{\r\s}$.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Consequently, we can write down the most general structure decomposition up to $O(\pt)$ for $\H^{\m\n}$ and $J^\m$ as follows~\footnote{One can start without including the co-moving time-derivative terms as those terms are eventually replaced by the spatial gradients up on using leading-order hydrodynamic EOMs.}
elements.sent-2411-055: But we keep them to make the discussions more transparent.
elements.sent-2411-056: They are constructed by decomposing first with respect to $u^\m$ and then with respect to different representation of $SO(3)$.
elements.sent-2411-057: In these decompositions, the terms with $f_0, m_0, n_0, o_0, p_0$ in $\H^{\m\n}$ and $F_0$ in $J^\m$ as coefficients transform differently from $\H^{\m\n}$ and $J^\m$ under parity (P), respectively, meaning that they can appear only when the system contains parity violating contents.
elements.sent-2411-058: Under time reversal transformation (T), all the terms of first-order gradients on the right-hand sides of $\H^{\m\n}$ and $J^\m$ except for terms with coefficients $f_0, m_0, n_0, o_0, p_0, F_0$ transform differently from $\H^{\m\n}$ and $J^\m$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-059: This means that these terms must be dissipative (that is to say, these terms are responsible for entropy generation in the fluid), while terms with coefficients $f_0, m_0, n_0, o_0, p_0, F_0$ can appear without generating entropy, i.e., they could arise in ideal hydrodynamics despite they are at first order in gradients.
elements.sent-2411-060: The terms with coefficients $f_0, m_0, n_0, o_0, p_0, F_0$ are thus especially interesting.
elements.sent-2411-061: In fact, some of them have been intensively studied and it was found that they contain very rich quantum phenomena (usually dubbed chiral anomalous transports) closely related to chiral anomaly of the system if the underlying physics is governed by quantum gauge theory.
elements.sent-2411-062: Recently, such chiral anomalous transports have been an active subjects in condensed matter physics, astrophysics, and heavy-ion collision physics; see Refs.~\cite{Kharzeev:2015znc,Huang:2015oca,Hattori:2016emy,Liu:2020ymh,Kharzeev:2020jxw,Kharzeev:2024zzm for recently reviews with a focus on heavy-ion collision physics.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Similarly, we could also examine the balance of right-hand and left-hand sides of \eqs{tmn:exp {j:exp under charge conjugation (C) transformation.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The terms with coefficients $b_0^n, d_0, h_0^n, k_0, o_0, A_0, B_0^\ve, B_0^u, C_0, E_0, F_0$ must vanish if there is no environmental charge-conjugation violation (Naturally, the presence of a nonzero charge density $n$ violates the C symmetry and allows these terms to present).
elements.sent-2411-065: The antisymmetric terms in $\H^{\m\n}$ are also particularly interesting.
elements.sent-2411-068: Thus the antisymmetric part of $\H^{\m\n}$ provide a source for the generation of spin (one may more clearly see this by integrating \eq{con:Mmnr2 over space).}
elements.sent-2411-069: This will be the focus of this article and we will come back to it from the next section.

elements.sent-2411-218: For the purpose of applying spin hydrodynamics to specific physical systems, we need to know what are the appropriate observables for detection of the spin degrees of freedom in the fluid.
elements.sent-2411-219: In principle, the presence of the spin degree of freedom in the fluid should modify the usual hydrodynamic quantities such as the energy density and fluid velocity, but when the spin density is not large (nevertheless it is always suppressed by $\hbar$ comparing to the traditional hydrodynamic quantities), such modification is small.
elements.sent-2411-220: In heavy ion collisions, the natural observable is the spin polarization of hadrons, including spin-1/2 hyperons and spin-1 vector mesons.
elements.sent-2411-221: Hyperons are of special interest because they primarily decay via weak interactions in such a way that the momentum of one of the daughter particles tends to align with the spin direction of the hyperon.
elements.sent-2411-222: In order to obtain the spin polarization observables of hadron from the spin hydrodynamics, we need a machinery to convert the outcomes of spin hydrodynamics, such as fluid velocity, temperature, and spin potential, to measurable hadronic observables.
elements.sent-2411-223: In the application of traditional hydrodynamics to heavy-ion collisions, the hadron momentum spectra are typically obtained using the so-called Cooper-Frye formula, \begin{eqnarray}\label{cooperfusual} E_p\frac{d N_i}{d\bp^3} = \int_\Xi d\Xi_\m(x) p^\m f_i(x,p),\end{eqnarray} where the integral is over the freeze-out hypersurface (where particlization occurs) $\Xi$, and $f_i(x,p)$ is the distribution function of species $i$ of the hadrons in the fluid.
elements.sent-2411-224: Any possible degeneracy of hadrons should be accounted for in $f_i$.
elements.sent-2411-225: For example, when dissipative effects are neglected, the distribution function $f_i$ is typically taken as the Fermi-Dirac or Bose-Einstein functions $f_{F,B (p\cdot \b-\m_i)$ with $\m_i$ the chemical potential.}
elements.sent-2411-226: The above Cooper-Frye formula has been widely used in hydrodynamic simulations in heavy-ion collisions and has proven to be very successful.

elements.sent-2411-273: In this article, we provide a pedagogical introduction to relativistic spin hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-274: We begin by demonstrating how one can derive a set of hydrodynamic equations from the conservation equations based on the requirement of local thermodynamic laws, primarily the second law of thermodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-275: We then extend this framework to include the conservation of angular momentum, which leads to spin hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-276: In the framework of spin hydrodynamics, the new (quasi-)hydrodynamic variable is the spin density.
elements.sent-2411-277: Due to spin-orbit coupling, the spin density is not a strict hydrodynamic variable but rather a quasi-hydrodynamic variable.
elements.sent-2411-278: It relaxes to a local equilibrium value determined by the local thermal vorticity, through the dissipative conversion of spin and orbital angular momenta.
elements.sent-2411-279: We show how such dissipative processes are characterized by two new transport coefficients: one for boost heat conductivity and another for rotational viscosity.
elements.sent-2411-280: We discuss several interesting aspects of spin hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-281: First, we address the pseudo-gauge ambiguity in defining the spin tensor, which reflects the freedom in separating the total angular momentum into its spin and orbital components.
elements.sent-2411-282: One consequence of this pseudo-gauge ambiguity is that we have the flexibility to choose spin tensors with different symmetries in their indices as the starting point for derivation of the spin hydrodynamics, leading to different constitutive relations.
elements.sent-2411-283: Second, we emphasize the importance of derivative power counting in the formulation of spin hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-284: In particular, for a strongly vortical (or strongly spin-polarized) fluid, it is natural to assign the vorticity and the spin potential as being of similar strength to other local thermodynamic quantities, such as temperature, in terms of derivative powers.
elements.sent-2411-286: As a result, anisotropy emerges in the constitutive relations both at zeroth order and first order in derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-287: This framework is well-suited for describing strongly vortical or spin-polarized fluids.
elements.sent-2411-288: Third, for potential applications of spin hydrodynamics, such as in heavy-ion collisions, we require a method to convert the results of spin hydrodynamics---specifically, the spin density (or spin potential), temperature, and fluid velocity---into momentum-space observables.
elements.sent-2411-289: To this end, we give a spin Cooper-Frye formula for Dirac fermions, and a similar formula can also be derived for spin-one vector bosons.
elements.sent-2411-290: Spin hydrodynamics is an area of intensive study, with many interesting aspects already explored and many more awaiting investigation.
elements.sent-2411-291: We provide a brief discussion of some of these topics.

elements.sent-2411-310: As we have seen, new transport coefficients appear in spin hydrodynamics, most notably the rotational viscosity $\eta_s$.
elements.sent-2411-311: Strictly speaking, $\eta_s$, unlike the usual shear viscosity $\eta$, is not a transport coefficient in the traditional sense.
elements.sent-2411-312: It does not characterize the ability to transport spin within the fluid; rather, it represents how quickly the spin density relaxes to its equilibrium value determined by thermal vorticity.

elements.sent-2411-320: In order to apply spin hydrodynamics to, for example, heavy-ion collisions, it is important to develop a suitable numerical framework for performing simulations.
elements.sent-2411-321: It is well known that first-order relativistic hydrodynamic equations suffer from numerical instabilities and the emergence of acausal modes.
elements.sent-2411-322: The origin of this problem lies in the fact that first-order constitutive relations are non-dynamical, meaning that the response of the fluid to thermodynamic forces is instantaneous.
elements.sent-2411-323: One solution to this problem is to make the constitutive relations dynamical.
elements.sent-2411-325: For relativistic spin hydrodynamics, to implement numerical simulations, one may adopt similar modifications to the constitutive relations.
elements.sent-2411-326: This has been recently discussed in Refs.~\cite{Liu:2020ymh,Weickgenannt:2022zxs,Weickgenannt:2022qvh,Biswas:2023qsw,Xie:2023gbo,Daher:2024bah,Wagner:2024fry .}
elements.sent-2411-328: With these modifications, a numerical simulation of relativistic spin hydrodynamics can be performed, which will provide valuable insights into spin polarization phenomena, such as those observed in heavy-ion collisions.
